title.raw: 《田敬仲完世家》 House of Tian Jingzhong

sentences.zh: 陳完者，陳厲公他之子也。

translations.literal: Chen Wan was the son of Chen Li Gong Ta.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Wan was the son of Duke Li of Chen, whose personal name was Ta.

sentences.zh: 完生，周太史過陳，陳厲公使卜完，卦得觀之否：「是為觀國之光，利用賓于王。

translations.literal: When Wan was born, the Grand Astrologer of Zhou passed through Chen. Duke Li of Chen had him divine for Wan. The hexagram obtained was Guan changing to Pi: 'This means observing the glory of the state, advantageous to be a guest of the king.'
translations.idiomatic: When Wan was born, the Grand Astrologer of Zhou happened to pass through the state of Chen. Duke Li had him cast a divination for the child. The hexagram obtained was Guan changing to Pi, and the reading declared: 'This signifies one who shall observe the glory of a state, and it is auspicious for him to serve as a guest of a king.'

sentences.zh: 此其代陳有國乎？

translations.literal: Will this one replace Chen and possess a state?
translations.idiomatic: Could it be that his line will one day supplant Chen and possess a state of its own?

sentences.zh: 不在此而在異國乎？

translations.literal: Not in this state but in a different state?
translations.idiomatic: Yet perhaps not in this state, but in a foreign land?

sentences.zh: 非此其身也，在其子孫。

translations.literal: Not in this person himself, but in his descendants.
translations.idiomatic: It will not come to pass in his own lifetime, but through his descendants.

sentences.zh: 若在異國，必姜姓。

translations.literal: If in a different state, it must be a state of the Jiang surname.
translations.idiomatic: If it is to be in a foreign state, then it must be one ruled by the Jiang clan.

sentences.zh: 姜姓，四嶽之後。

translations.literal: The Jiang surname are descendants of the Four Peaks.
translations.idiomatic: The Jiang are descendants of the Four Peaks.

sentences.zh: 物莫能兩大，陳衰，此其昌乎？」

translations.literal: No thing can have two greats; when Chen declines, this one shall flourish.
translations.idiomatic: Nothing in this world can sustain two great powers at once — when Chen declines, his line shall surely flourish."

sentences.zh: 厲公者，陳文公少子也，其母蔡女。

translations.literal: Duke Li was the youngest son of Duke Wen of Chen; his mother was a woman of Cai.
translations.idiomatic: Duke Li was the youngest son of Duke Wen of Chen, and his mother was a woman from the state of Cai.

sentences.zh: 文公卒，厲公兄鮑立，是為桓公。

translations.literal: When Duke Wen died, Duke Li's elder brother Bao was enthroned; he was Duke Huan.
translations.idiomatic: When Duke Wen died, Duke Li's elder brother Bao ascended the throne and became Duke Huan.

sentences.zh: 桓公與他異母。

translations.literal: Duke Huan and Ta had different mothers.
translations.idiomatic: Duke Huan and Ta were born of different mothers.

sentences.zh: 及桓公病，蔡人為他殺桓公鮑及太子免而立他，為厲公。

translations.literal: When Duke Huan fell ill, the Cai people on behalf of Ta killed Duke Huan Bao and Crown Prince Mian, and enthroned Ta as Duke Li.
translations.idiomatic: When Duke Huan fell ill, men of Cai acting on Ta's behalf killed Duke Huan Bao and Crown Prince Mian, then placed Ta on the throne as Duke Li.

sentences.zh: 厲公既立，娶蔡女。

translations.literal: After Duke Li was enthroned, he married a woman of Cai.
translations.idiomatic: Once Duke Li was established on the throne, he took a woman of Cai as his wife.

sentences.zh: 蔡女淫於蔡人，數歸，厲公亦數如蔡。

translations.literal: The Cai woman was licentious with Cai men, frequently returning home; Duke Li also frequently went to Cai.
translations.idiomatic: The woman of Cai carried on illicit affairs with men of her homeland, often returning to Cai, and Duke Li likewise traveled there frequently.

sentences.zh: 桓公之少子林怨厲公殺其父與兄，乃令蔡人誘厲公而殺之。

translations.literal: Duke Huan's youngest son Lin resented Duke Li for killing his father and brother, so he ordered the Cai people to lure Duke Li and kill him.
translations.idiomatic: Lin, the youngest son of Duke Huan, harbored deep resentment against Duke Li for the murder of his father and brother, and so he had men of Cai lure Duke Li into a trap and kill him.

sentences.zh: 林自立，是為莊公。

translations.literal: Lin enthroned himself; he was Duke Zhuang.
translations.idiomatic: Lin then installed himself on the throne and became Duke Zhuang.

sentences.zh: 故陳完不得立，為陳大夫。

translations.literal: Therefore Chen Wan could not be enthroned, and served as a grandee of Chen.
translations.idiomatic: As a result, Chen Wan was unable to claim the throne and served instead as a grandee of Chen.

sentences.zh: 厲公之殺，以淫出國，故《春秋》曰「蔡人殺陳他」，罪之也。

translations.literal: Duke Li's killing — because of licentiousness leaving the state — therefore the Spring and Autumn Annals says 'the Cai people killed Chen Ta,' condemning him.
translations.idiomatic: Duke Li was killed because his debauchery led him away from his own state. For this reason, the Spring and Autumn Annals records that 'the men of Cai killed Chen Ta,' placing the blame on him.

sentences.zh: 莊公卒，立弟杵臼，是為宣公。

translations.literal: Duke Zhuang died; his younger brother Chu Jiu was enthroned, becoming Duke Xuan.
translations.idiomatic: When Duke Zhuang died, his younger brother Chu Jiu was placed on the throne and became Duke Xuan.

sentences.zh: 宣公［二］十一年，殺其太子御寇。

translations.literal: In the twenty-first year of Duke Xuan, he killed his crown prince Yu Kou.
translations.idiomatic: In the twenty-first year of Duke Xuan's reign, the duke had his own crown prince, Yu Kou, put to death.

sentences.zh: 御寇與完相愛，恐禍及己，完故奔齊。

translations.literal: Yu Kou and Wan were close friends; fearing disaster would reach himself, Wan therefore fled to Qi.
translations.idiomatic: Yu Kou and Wan had been close friends, and Wan, fearing that the calamity might engulf him as well, fled to the state of Qi.

sentences.zh: 齊桓公欲使為卿，辭曰：「羈旅之臣幸得免負檐，君之惠也，不敢當高位。」

translations.literal: Duke Huan of Qi wished to make him a minister, but he declined saying: 'A sojourning subject is fortunate to be spared carrying loads on his back — this is the lord's grace; I dare not accept a high position.'
translations.idiomatic: Duke Huan of Qi wished to appoint him as a minister, but Wan declined, saying: "A wandering exile like myself is fortunate enough simply to be spared the burden of a refugee's life — such is my lord's generosity. I dare not presume to accept so high a position."

sentences.zh: 桓公使為工正。

translations.literal: Duke Huan appointed him as Supervisor of Artisans.
translations.idiomatic: Duke Huan then appointed him Supervisor of Artisans.

sentences.zh: 齊懿仲欲妻完，卜之，占曰：「是謂鳳皇于蜚，和鳴鏘鏘。

translations.literal: Yi Zhong of Qi wished to marry his daughter to Wan; he divined about it. The divination said: 'This is called the phoenix in flight, singing in harmony with resonant tones.'
translations.idiomatic: Yi Zhong of Qi wished to give his daughter in marriage to Wan and cast a divination on the matter. The oracle declared: "This is the phoenix in flight, its harmonious song ringing clear and bright."

sentences.zh: 有媯之後，將育于姜。

translations.literal: A descendant of the Gui clan will be nurtured among the Jiang.
translations.idiomatic: A scion of the Gui clan shall be nurtured among the Jiang.

sentences.zh: 五世其昌，并于正卿。

translations.literal: In five generations it shall flourish, reaching the rank of chief minister.
translations.idiomatic: Within five generations his line shall flourish, rising to the rank of chief minister.

sentences.zh: 八世之後，莫之與京。」

translations.literal: After eight generations, none shall rival them.
translations.idiomatic: After eight generations, none shall rival their greatness."

sentences.zh: 卒妻完。

translations.literal: In the end he married his daughter to Wan.
translations.idiomatic: And so he gave his daughter in marriage to Wan.

sentences.zh: 完之奔齊，齊桓公立十四年矣。

translations.literal: When Wan fled to Qi, Duke Huan of Qi had been on the throne for fourteen years.
translations.idiomatic: At the time of Wan's flight to Qi, Duke Huan had already reigned for fourteen years.

sentences.zh: 完卒，謚為敬仲。

translations.literal: When Wan died, his posthumous name was Jing Zhong.
translations.idiomatic: When Wan died, he was given the posthumous name Jingzhong.

sentences.zh: 仲生稚孟夷。

translations.literal: Zhong fathered Zhi Meng Yi.
translations.idiomatic: Jingzhong fathered a son named Zhi Meng Yi.

sentences.zh: 敬仲之如齊，以陳字為田氏。

translations.literal: When Jingzhong went to Qi, he took the character Chen and changed it to the Tian surname.
translations.idiomatic: When Jingzhong settled in Qi, he adopted the surname Tian, derived from the name Chen.

sentences.zh: 田稚孟夷生湣孟莊，田湣孟莊生文子須無。

translations.literal: Tian Zhi Meng Yi fathered Min Meng Zhuang; Tian Min Meng Zhuang fathered Wenzi Xu Wu.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Zhi Meng Yi fathered Min Meng Zhuang, who in turn fathered Wenzi Xu Wu.

sentences.zh: 田文子事齊莊公。

translations.literal: Tian Wenzi served Duke Zhuang of Qi.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Wenzi served Duke Zhuang of Qi.

sentences.zh: 晉之大夫欒逞作亂於晉，來奔齊，齊莊公厚客之。

translations.literal: The Jin grandee Luan Cheng made rebellion in Jin, came and fled to Qi; Duke Zhuang of Qi received him generously as a guest.
translations.idiomatic: Luan Cheng, a grandee of Jin, raised a rebellion in his own state and fled to Qi, where Duke Zhuang received him with lavish hospitality.

sentences.zh: 晏嬰與田文子諫，莊公弗聽。

translations.literal: Yan Ying and Tian Wenzi remonstrated, but Duke Zhuang did not listen.
translations.idiomatic: Yan Ying and Tian Wenzi both counseled against this, but Duke Zhuang would not listen.

sentences.zh: 文子卒，生桓子無宇。

translations.literal: Wenzi died; he had fathered Huanzi Wu Yu.
translations.idiomatic: When Wenzi died, he was succeeded by his son Huanzi Wu Yu.

sentences.zh: 田桓子無宇有力，事齊莊公，甚有寵。

translations.literal: Tian Huanzi Wu Yu was strong; he served Duke Zhuang of Qi and was greatly favored.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Huanzi Wu Yu was a man of great strength who served Duke Zhuang of Qi and enjoyed his particular favor.

sentences.zh: 無宇卒，生武子開與釐子乞。

translations.literal: Wu Yu died; he had fathered Wuzi Kai and Xizi Qi.
translations.idiomatic: When Wu Yu died, he left behind two sons: Wuzi Kai and Xizi Qi.

sentences.zh: 田釐子乞事齊景公為大夫，其收賦稅於民以小斗受之，其（粟）［稟］予民以大斗，行陰德於民，而景公弗禁。

translations.literal: Tian Xizi Qi served Duke Jing of Qi as a grandee. He collected taxes from the people using a small measure, and distributed grain to the people using a large measure, secretly performing benevolence upon the people, and Duke Jing did not prohibit it.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Xizi Qi served Duke Jing of Qi as a grandee. When he collected taxes from the people, he used a small measure, but when he distributed grain to them, he used a large one. In this way he quietly bestowed favors upon the common people, and Duke Jing did nothing to stop it.

sentences.zh: 由此田氏得齊眾心，宗族益彊，民思田氏。

translations.literal: Through this the Tian clan won the hearts of the Qi people; the clan grew ever stronger, and the people yearned for the Tian.
translations.idiomatic: Through this the Tian clan won the hearts of the people of Qi. Their kinsmen grew ever more powerful, and the common folk looked to the Tian with gratitude.

sentences.zh: 晏子數諫景公，景公弗聽。

translations.literal: Yanzi repeatedly remonstrated with Duke Jing, but Duke Jing did not listen.
translations.idiomatic: Yanzi remonstrated with Duke Jing on numerous occasions, but the duke refused to listen.

sentences.zh: 已而使於晉，與叔向私語曰：「齊國之政卒歸於田氏矣。」

translations.literal: Later, on a diplomatic mission to Jin, he spoke privately with Shu Xiang, saying: 'The governance of Qi will eventually fall to the Tian clan.'
translations.idiomatic: Later, while on a diplomatic mission to Jin, Yanzi confided to Shu Xiang in private: "The government of Qi will inevitably pass into the hands of the Tian clan."

sentences.zh: 晏嬰卒後，范、中行氏反晉。

translations.literal: After Yan Ying died, the Fan and Zhonghang clans rebelled against Jin.
translations.idiomatic: After Yan Ying's death, the Fan and Zhonghang clans rose in rebellion against Jin.

sentences.zh: 晉攻之急，范、中行請粟於齊。

translations.literal: Jin attacked them urgently; Fan and Zhonghang requested grain from Qi.
translations.idiomatic: Jin pressed its attack upon them with great urgency, and the Fan and Zhonghang clans turned to Qi to request grain.

sentences.zh: 田乞欲為亂，樹黨於諸侯，乃說景公曰：「范、中行數有德於齊，齊不可不救。」

translations.literal: Tian Qi wished to create disorder and build alliances among the feudal lords, so he persuaded Duke Jing, saying: 'The Fan and Zhonghang clans have often been benefactors to Qi; Qi must not fail to rescue them.'
translations.idiomatic: Tian Qi, harboring ambitions of his own, sought to build alliances among the feudal lords. He persuaded Duke Jing by arguing: "The Fan and Zhonghang clans have shown great kindness to Qi on many occasions. We cannot in good conscience refuse to come to their aid."

sentences.zh: 齊使田乞救之而輸之粟。

translations.literal: Qi sent Tian Qi to rescue them and deliver grain to them.
translations.idiomatic: Qi then dispatched Tian Qi to rescue them and supply them with grain.

sentences.zh: 景公太子死，後有寵姬曰芮子，生子荼。

translations.literal: Duke Jing's crown prince died. Later a favored consort called Ruizi bore a son named Tu.
translations.idiomatic: Duke Jing's crown prince had died. Among his favored consorts was one called Ruizi, who bore him a son named Tu.

sentences.zh: 景公病，命其相國惠子與高昭子以子荼為太子。

translations.literal: When Duke Jing fell ill, he commanded his ministers Guo Huizi and Gao Zhaozi to make Tu the crown prince.
translations.idiomatic: When Duke Jing fell gravely ill, he entrusted his ministers Guo Huizi and Gao Zhaozi with the charge of establishing Tu as crown prince.

sentences.zh: 景公卒，兩相高、國立荼，是為晏孺子。

translations.literal: Duke Jing died; the two ministers Gao and Guo enthroned Tu, who became Yan Ruzi.
translations.idiomatic: After Duke Jing died, the two ministers of the Gao and Guo clans placed Tu on the throne. He was known as Yan Ruzi.

sentences.zh: 而田乞不說，欲立景公他子陽生。

translations.literal: But Tian Qi was displeased and wished to enthrone another of Duke Jing's sons, Yang Sheng.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Qi, however, was displeased and wished instead to place Yang Sheng, another of Duke Jing's sons, on the throne.

sentences.zh: 陽生素與乞歡。

translations.literal: Yang Sheng had always been on good terms with Qi.
translations.idiomatic: Yang Sheng and Tian Qi had long been on friendly terms.

sentences.zh: 晏孺子之立也，陽生奔魯。

translations.literal: When Yan Ruzi was enthroned, Yang Sheng fled to Lu.
translations.idiomatic: When Yan Ruzi was placed on the throne, Yang Sheng fled to the state of Lu.

sentences.zh: 田乞偽事高昭子、國惠子者，每朝代參乘，言曰：「始諸大夫不欲立孺子。

translations.literal: Tian Qi pretended to serve Gao Zhaozi and Guo Huizi loyally, and at every court session, riding in their carriage, he would say: 'From the beginning, the various grandees did not wish to enthrone Ruzi.'
translations.idiomatic: Tian Qi feigned loyal service to Gao Zhaozi and Guo Huizi, and at every court session, while riding with them in their chariot, he would whisper: "From the very beginning, the grandees were opposed to placing Ruzi on the throne."

sentences.zh: 孺子既立，君相之，大夫皆自危，謀作亂。」

translations.literal: Now that Ruzi has been enthroned and you serve as his ministers, the grandees all feel endangered and plot rebellion.
translations.idiomatic: "Now that Ruzi is on the throne and you serve as his ministers, every grandee fears for his own safety and plots rebellion."

sentences.zh: 又紿大夫曰：「高昭子可畏也，及未發先之。」

translations.literal: He also deceived the grandees, saying: 'Gao Zhaozi is to be feared; strike before he acts first.'
translations.idiomatic: He also deceived the grandees, telling them: "Gao Zhaozi is a man to be feared — you must strike before he moves against you."

sentences.zh: 諸大夫從之。

translations.literal: The grandees followed him.
translations.idiomatic: The grandees were persuaded and followed his lead.

sentences.zh: 田乞、鮑牧與大夫以兵入公室，攻高昭子。

translations.literal: Tian Qi, Bao Mu, and the grandees entered the ducal palace with troops and attacked Gao Zhaozi.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Qi, Bao Mu, and the grandees then led their armed men into the ducal palace and launched an attack on Gao Zhaozi.

sentences.zh: 昭子聞之，與國惠子救公。

translations.literal: When Zhaozi heard this, he and Guo Huizi went to rescue the duke.
translations.idiomatic: When Zhaozi learned of the attack, he and Guo Huizi rushed to defend the duke.

sentences.zh: 公師敗。

translations.literal: The duke's forces were defeated.
translations.idiomatic: The duke's forces were defeated.

sentences.zh: 田乞之眾追國惠子，惠子奔莒，遂返殺高昭子。

translations.literal: Tian Qi's followers pursued Guo Huizi; Huizi fled to Ju. They then returned and killed Gao Zhaozi.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Qi's men pursued Guo Huizi, who fled to the state of Ju. They then turned back and killed Gao Zhaozi.

sentences.zh: 晏（孺子）［圉］奔魯。

translations.literal: Yan Yu fled to Lu.
translations.idiomatic: Yan Yu fled to the state of Lu.

sentences.zh: 田乞使人之魯，迎陽生。

translations.literal: Tian Qi sent men to Lu to welcome Yang Sheng.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Qi then sent men to Lu to escort Yang Sheng back.

sentences.zh: 陽生至齊，匿田乞家。

translations.literal: Yang Sheng arrived in Qi and hid at Tian Qi's house.
translations.idiomatic: When Yang Sheng arrived in Qi, he was hidden in Tian Qi's household.

sentences.zh: 請諸大夫曰：「常之母有魚菽之祭，幸而來會飲。」

translations.literal: He invited the grandees saying: 'Chang's mother is holding a sacrifice of fish and beans; please come and join the drinking.'
translations.idiomatic: Tian Qi then invited the grandees, saying: "Chang's mother is holding a sacrificial feast of fish and beans. Would you kindly come and join us for a drink?"

sentences.zh: 會飲田氏。

translations.literal: They gathered to drink at the Tian household.
translations.idiomatic: The grandees gathered to drink at the Tian residence.

sentences.zh: 田乞盛陽生橐中，置坐中央。

translations.literal: Tian Qi placed Yang Sheng inside a large sack and set it in the center of the seats.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Qi had concealed Yang Sheng inside a large sack and placed it in the center of the gathering.

sentences.zh: 發橐，出陽生，曰：「此乃齊君矣。」

translations.literal: He opened the sack, brought Yang Sheng out, and said: 'This is the lord of Qi.'
translations.idiomatic: He then opened the sack, revealed Yang Sheng, and declared: "Here is the rightful lord of Qi!"

sentences.zh: 大夫皆伏謁。

translations.literal: The grandees all prostrated themselves in obeisance.
translations.idiomatic: All the grandees prostrated themselves in submission.

sentences.zh: 將盟立之，田乞誣曰：「吾與鮑牧謀共立陽生也。」

translations.literal: As they were about to swear an oath to enthrone him, Tian Qi falsely claimed: 'I and Bao Mu together planned to enthrone Yang Sheng.'
translations.idiomatic: As they prepared to swear an oath of allegiance, Tian Qi falsely declared: "Bao Mu and I planned this together — we jointly resolved to place Yang Sheng on the throne."

sentences.zh: 鮑牧怒曰：「大夫忘景公之命乎？」

translations.literal: Bao Mu angrily said: 'Have the grandees forgotten Duke Jing's command?'
translations.idiomatic: Bao Mu was furious and exclaimed: "Have the grandees forgotten the dying command of Duke Jing?"

sentences.zh: 諸大夫欲悔，陽生乃頓首曰：「可則立之，不可則已。」

translations.literal: The grandees wished to reconsider. Yang Sheng then kowtowed and said: 'If it is acceptable, enthrone me; if not, let it be.'
translations.idiomatic: The grandees began to waver. Yang Sheng then prostrated himself and said: "If you find it acceptable, make me your lord. If not, let the matter rest."

sentences.zh: 鮑牧恐禍及己，乃復曰：「皆景公之子，何為不可！」

translations.literal: Bao Mu feared disaster would reach himself, so he relented and said: 'They are all sons of Duke Jing; why should it not be acceptable!'
translations.idiomatic: Bao Mu, fearing that the calamity might turn upon himself, relented and said: "They are all sons of Duke Jing — what reason is there to object?"

sentences.zh: 遂立陽生於田乞之家，是為悼公。

translations.literal: They then enthroned Yang Sheng at Tian Qi's home; he became Duke Dao.
translations.idiomatic: And so Yang Sheng was enthroned at the home of Tian Qi, becoming Duke Dao.

sentences.zh: 乃使人遷晏孺子於駘，而殺孺子荼。

translations.literal: They then sent men to relocate Yan Ruzi to Tai, and killed the young lord Tu.
translations.idiomatic: They then had Yan Ruzi removed to Tai, where the young lord Tu was put to death.

sentences.zh: 悼公既立，田乞為相，專齊政。

translations.literal: After Duke Dao was enthroned, Tian Qi became chief minister and monopolized the government of Qi.
translations.idiomatic: Once Duke Dao was established on the throne, Tian Qi became chief minister and took sole control of the government of Qi.

sentences.zh: 四年，田乞卒，子常代立，是為田成子。

translations.literal: In the fourth year, Tian Qi died. His son Chang succeeded him; he was Tian Chengzi.
translations.idiomatic: In the fourth year of Duke Dao's reign, Tian Qi died and was succeeded by his son Chang, who became known as Tian Chengzi.

sentences.zh: 鮑牧與齊悼公有郄，弒悼公。

translations.literal: Bao Mu had a quarrel with Duke Dao of Qi and assassinated Duke Dao.
translations.idiomatic: Bao Mu fell into a dispute with Duke Dao and assassinated him.

sentences.zh: 齊人共立其子壬，是為簡公。

translations.literal: The people of Qi together enthroned his son Ren, who became Duke Jian.
translations.idiomatic: The people of Qi then jointly placed the duke's son Ren on the throne, and he became Duke Jian.

sentences.zh: 田常成子與監止俱為左右相，相簡公。

translations.literal: Tian Chang Chengzi and Jian Zhi both served as left and right ministers, serving Duke Jian.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Chang, styled Chengzi, and Jian Zhi served together as the left and right chief ministers under Duke Jian.

sentences.zh: 田常心害監止，監止幸於簡公，權弗能去。

translations.literal: Tian Chang secretly wished to harm Jian Zhi, but Jian Zhi was favored by Duke Jian, and his power could not be removed.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Chang secretly wished to do away with Jian Zhi, but Jian Zhi enjoyed Duke Jian's favor, and his influence could not easily be dislodged.

sentences.zh: 於是田常復修釐子之政，以大斗出貸，以小斗收。

translations.literal: So Tian Chang once again adopted the policies of Xizi, lending with a large measure and collecting with a small one.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Chang therefore revived the policies of his grandfather Xizi, lending grain with a large measure and collecting repayment with a small one.

sentences.zh: 齊人歌之曰：「嫗乎采芑，歸乎田成子！」

translations.literal: The people of Qi sang of it: 'Old woman picking millet, bring it home to Tian Chengzi!'
translations.idiomatic: The people of Qi sang about it: "Old woman gathering grain, bring your harvest home to Tian Chengzi!"

sentences.zh: 齊大夫朝，御鞅諫簡公曰：「田、監不可并也，君其擇焉。」

translations.literal: At the court of the Qi grandees, Yu Yang remonstrated with Duke Jian: 'The Tian and Jian cannot coexist; my lord should choose between them.'
translations.idiomatic: At court, the grandee Yu Yang admonished Duke Jian: "The Tian and the Jian cannot coexist. My lord must choose between them."

sentences.zh: 君弗聽。

translations.literal: The lord did not listen.
translations.idiomatic: But the duke would not listen.

sentences.zh: 子我者，監止之宗人也，常與田氏有卻。

translations.literal: Zi Wo was a clansman of Jian Zhi and was always at odds with the Tian clan.
translations.idiomatic: Zi Wo was a kinsman of Jian Zhi and had long been in conflict with the Tian clan.

sentences.zh: 田氏疏族田豹事子我有寵。

translations.literal: Tian Bao, a distant relative of the Tian clan, served Zi Wo and was favored by him.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Bao, a distant kinsman of the Tian clan, served Zi Wo and enjoyed his particular favor.

sentences.zh: 子我曰：「吾欲盡滅田氏適，以豹代田氏宗。」

translations.literal: Zi Wo said: 'I wish to completely destroy the Tian clan's main line, and have Bao replace them as head of the Tian clan.'
translations.idiomatic: Zi Wo said: "I intend to exterminate the main branch of the Tian clan entirely and install Bao as the head of the Tian family."

sentences.zh: 豹曰：「臣於田氏疏矣。」

translations.literal: Bao said: 'I am but a distant relative of the Tian clan.'
translations.idiomatic: Bao replied: "I am only a distant relative of the Tian. This would not be proper."

sentences.zh: 不聽。

translations.literal: He did not listen.
translations.idiomatic: But Zi Wo would not heed his objection.

sentences.zh: 已而豹謂田氏曰：「子我將誅田氏，田氏弗先，禍及矣。」

translations.literal: Later, Bao told the Tian clan: 'Zi Wo plans to destroy the Tian clan. If the Tian do not act first, disaster will come.'
translations.idiomatic: Before long, however, Bao went to the Tian clan and warned them: "Zi Wo intends to destroy the Tian. If you do not strike first, ruin will be upon you."

sentences.zh: 子我舍公宮，田常兄弟四人乘如公宮，欲殺子我。

translations.literal: Zi Wo was lodging in the ducal palace. Tian Chang and his three brothers rode to the ducal palace, intending to kill Zi Wo.
translations.idiomatic: Zi Wo was staying in the ducal palace. Tian Chang and three of his brothers rode to the palace, determined to kill him.

sentences.zh: 子我閉門。

translations.literal: Zi Wo shut the gates.
translations.idiomatic: Zi Wo barred the gates.

sentences.zh: 簡公與婦人飲檀臺，將欲擊田常。

translations.literal: Duke Jian was drinking with women at the Altar Terrace, and was about to attack Tian Chang.
translations.idiomatic: Duke Jian was drinking with his women at the Tan Terrace and was preparing to strike against Tian Chang.

sentences.zh: 太史子餘曰：「田常非敢為亂，將除害。」

translations.literal: The Grand Scribe Zi Yu said: 'Tian Chang does not dare create disorder; he means to remove harm.'
translations.idiomatic: The Grand Scribe Zi Yu interceded, saying: "Tian Chang is not attempting to create disorder — he seeks only to remove a threat."

sentences.zh: 簡公乃止。

translations.literal: Duke Jian then stopped.
translations.idiomatic: Duke Jian relented and held back.

sentences.zh: 田常出，聞簡公怒，恐誅，將出亡。

translations.literal: When Tian Chang emerged, he heard that Duke Jian was angry, feared execution, and was about to flee.
translations.idiomatic: When Tian Chang emerged and learned of Duke Jian's fury, he feared for his life and prepared to flee.

sentences.zh: 田子行曰：「需，事之賊也。」

translations.literal: Tian Zi Xing said: 'Hesitation is the thief of enterprise.'
translations.idiomatic: Tian Zixing counseled him: "Hesitation is the enemy of action."

sentences.zh: 田常於是擊子我。

translations.literal: Tian Chang then attacked Zi Wo.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Chang thereupon launched his attack on Zi Wo.

sentences.zh: 子我率其徒攻田氏，不勝，出亡。

translations.literal: Zi Wo led his followers to attack the Tian clan but was defeated and fled.
translations.idiomatic: Zi Wo rallied his followers to counterattack the Tian, but he was defeated and forced to flee.

sentences.zh: 田氏之徒追殺子我及監止。

translations.literal: The followers of the Tian clan pursued and killed Zi Wo and Jian Zhi.
translations.idiomatic: The Tian followers pursued and killed both Zi Wo and Jian Zhi.

sentences.zh: 簡公出奔，田氏之徒追執簡公于徐州。

translations.literal: Duke Jian fled; the followers of the Tian clan pursued and captured Duke Jian at Xuzhou.
translations.idiomatic: Duke Jian attempted to flee, but the Tian followers pursued and captured him at Xuzhou.

sentences.zh: 簡公曰：「蚤從御鞅之言，不及此難。」

translations.literal: Duke Jian said: 'If I had followed Yu Yang's words earlier, I would not have come to this disaster.'
translations.idiomatic: Duke Jian lamented: "Had I heeded Yu Yang's counsel sooner, I would never have come to this."

sentences.zh: 田氏之徒恐簡公復立而誅己，遂殺簡公。

translations.literal: The Tian followers feared Duke Jian might be restored and punish them, so they killed Duke Jian.
translations.idiomatic: Fearing that Duke Jian might be restored to power and take vengeance upon them, the Tian followers put him to death.

sentences.zh: 簡公立四年而殺。

translations.literal: Duke Jian had reigned four years when he was killed.
translations.idiomatic: Duke Jian had reigned for four years when he was killed.

sentences.zh: 於是田常立簡公弟驁，是為平公。

translations.literal: Thereupon Tian Chang enthroned Duke Jian's younger brother Ao, who became Duke Ping.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Chang then placed Duke Jian's younger brother Ao on the throne, and he became Duke Ping.

sentences.zh: 平公即位，田常為相。

translations.literal: When Duke Ping ascended the throne, Tian Chang served as chief minister.
translations.idiomatic: With Duke Ping on the throne, Tian Chang served as chief minister.

sentences.zh: 田常既殺簡公，懼諸侯共誅己，乃盡歸魯、衛侵地，西約晉、韓、魏、趙氏，南通吳、越之使，修功行賞，親於百姓，以故齊復定。

translations.literal: Having killed Duke Jian, Tian Chang feared the feudal lords would unite to punish him, so he returned all lands seized from Lu and Wei, made western alliances with Jin, Han, Wei, and Zhao, established southern communication with the envoys of Wu and Yue, cultivated merit and bestowed rewards, and drew close to the common people. Thus Qi was again stabilized.
translations.idiomatic: Having killed Duke Jian, Tian Chang feared that the feudal lords would unite to punish him. He therefore returned all the territories that had been seized from Lu and Wei, forged western alliances with the houses of Jin, Han, Wei, and Zhao, and opened diplomatic ties with Wu and Yue to the south. He rewarded merit generously and cultivated the goodwill of the common people. Through these measures, the state of Qi was once again brought to order.

sentences.zh: 田常言於齊平公曰：「德施人之所欲，君其行之；

translations.literal: Tian Chang said to Duke Ping of Qi: 'Virtuous acts and beneficence are what people desire; let my lord perform them.'
translations.idiomatic: Tian Chang addressed Duke Ping, saying: "Acts of virtue and benevolence are what the people desire — let my lord bestow them."

sentences.zh: 刑罰人之所惡，臣請行之。」

translations.literal: Punishments and penalties are what people hate; let your servant carry them out.
translations.idiomatic: "Punishments and penalties are what the people abhor — let your servant carry those out."

sentences.zh: 行之五年，齊國之政皆歸田常。

translations.literal: After five years of this practice, all the governance of Qi returned to Tian Chang.
translations.idiomatic: After five years of this arrangement, the entire governance of Qi had fallen into the hands of Tian Chang.

sentences.zh: 田常於是盡誅鮑、晏、監止及公族之彊者，而割齊自安平以東至瑯邪，自為封邑。

translations.literal: Tian Chang then completely eliminated the Bao, Yan, and Jian Zhi clans as well as the powerful members of the ducal family, and carved out all of Qi from Anping eastward to Langya as his own fief.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Chang then exterminated the Bao, Yan, and Jian Zhi clans along with every powerful member of the ducal house, and carved out the territory from Anping eastward to Langya as his personal fief.

sentences.zh: 封邑大於平公之所食。

translations.literal: His fief was larger than what Duke Ping himself possessed.
translations.idiomatic: His fief was larger than the domain held by Duke Ping himself.

sentences.zh: 田常乃選齊國中女子長七尺以上為後宮，後宮以百數，而使賓客舍人出入後宮者不禁。

translations.literal: Tian Chang selected women from Qi who were seven feet or taller for his harem, numbering in the hundreds, and allowed guests and retainers to come and go in the harem without restriction.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Chang then selected women from across Qi who stood seven feet or taller to fill his harem, which numbered in the hundreds. He permitted his guests and retainers to come and go freely in the inner chambers without restriction.

sentences.zh: 及田常卒，有七十餘男。

translations.literal: By the time Tian Chang died, he had over seventy sons.
translations.idiomatic: By the time of Tian Chang's death, he had fathered more than seventy sons.

sentences.zh: 田常卒，子襄子盤代立，相齊。

translations.literal: When Tian Chang died, his son Xiangzi Pan succeeded him and served as chief minister of Qi.
translations.idiomatic: When Tian Chang died, his son Xiangzi Pan succeeded him and served as chief minister of Qi.

sentences.zh: 常謚為成子。

translations.literal: Chang was posthumously named Chengzi.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Chang was given the posthumous name Chengzi.

sentences.zh: 田襄子既相齊宣公，三晉殺知伯，分其地。

translations.literal: When Tian Xiangzi was serving as chief minister under Duke Xuan of Qi, the Three Jin killed Zhi Bo and divided his territory.
translations.idiomatic: While Tian Xiangzi served as chief minister under Duke Xuan of Qi, the Three Houses of Jin killed Zhi Bo and divided his lands among themselves.

sentences.zh: 襄子使其兄弟宗人盡為齊都邑大夫，與三晉通使，且以有齊國。

translations.literal: Xiangzi had his brothers and clansmen serve as grandees of all the cities of Qi, exchanged envoys with the Three Jin, and was on the verge of possessing the state of Qi.
translations.idiomatic: Xiangzi installed his brothers and kinsmen as grandees governing every major city in Qi, exchanged envoys with the Three Jin states, and steadily moved toward seizing the state of Qi for his own house.

sentences.zh: 襄子卒，子莊子白立。

translations.literal: Xiangzi died; his son Zhuangzi Bai succeeded him.
translations.idiomatic: When Xiangzi died, he was succeeded by his son Zhuangzi Bai.

sentences.zh: 田莊子相齊宣公。

translations.literal: Tian Zhuangzi served as chief minister of Duke Xuan of Qi.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Zhuangzi served as chief minister under Duke Xuan of Qi.

sentences.zh: 宣公四十三年，伐晉，毀黃城，圍陽狐。

translations.literal: In the forty-third year of Duke Xuan, they attacked Jin, destroyed Huang city, and besieged Yanghu.
translations.idiomatic: In the forty-third year of Duke Xuan's reign, Qi attacked Jin, destroyed the city of Huang, and laid siege to Yanghu.

sentences.zh: 明年，伐魯、葛及安陵。

translations.literal: The following year, they attacked Lu, Ge, and Anling.
translations.idiomatic: The following year, they attacked Lu and the towns of Ge and Anling.

sentences.zh: 明年，取魯之一城。

translations.literal: The following year, they took one city from Lu.
translations.idiomatic: The year after that, they captured a city from Lu.

sentences.zh: 莊子卒，子太公和立。

translations.literal: Zhuangzi died; his son Tai Gong He succeeded him.
translations.idiomatic: When Zhuangzi died, his son Tai Gong He succeeded him.

sentences.zh: 田太公相齊宣公。

translations.literal: Tian Tai Gong served as chief minister of Duke Xuan of Qi.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Tai Gong served as chief minister under Duke Xuan of Qi.

sentences.zh: 宣公四十八年，取魯之城。

translations.literal: In the forty-eighth year of Duke Xuan, they took a city from Lu.
translations.idiomatic: In the forty-eighth year of Duke Xuan's reign, they captured another city from Lu.

sentences.zh: 明年，宣公與鄭人會西城。

translations.literal: The following year, Duke Xuan met with the people of Zheng at Xicheng.
translations.idiomatic: The following year, Duke Xuan met with envoys from Zheng at Xicheng.

sentences.zh: 伐衛，取毋丘。

translations.literal: They attacked Wei and took Wuqiu.
translations.idiomatic: They attacked Wei and captured Wuqiu.

sentences.zh: 宣公五十一年卒，田會自廩丘反。

translations.literal: Duke Xuan died in his fifty-first year. Tian Hui rebelled from Linqiu.
translations.idiomatic: In the fifty-first year of his reign, Duke Xuan died. Tian Hui raised a rebellion from Linqiu.

sentences.zh: 宣公卒，子康公貸立。

translations.literal: When Duke Xuan died, his son Duke Kang Dai succeeded him.
translations.idiomatic: After Duke Xuan's death, his son Duke Kang, named Dai, succeeded to the throne.

sentences.zh: 貸立十四年，淫於酒婦人，不聽政。

translations.literal: After Dai had been on the throne for fourteen years, he indulged in wine and women and did not attend to governance.
translations.idiomatic: After fourteen years on the throne, Duke Kang had given himself over to wine and women and no longer attended to affairs of state.

sentences.zh: 太公乃遷康公於海上，食一城，以奉其先祀。

translations.literal: Tai Gong then relocated Duke Kang to the seashore, giving him one city to maintain the ancestral sacrifices.
translations.idiomatic: Tai Gong therefore removed Duke Kang to the seashore and granted him a single city for the maintenance of his ancestral sacrifices.

sentences.zh: 明年，魯敗齊平陸。

translations.literal: The following year, Lu defeated Qi at Pinglu.
translations.idiomatic: The following year, Lu defeated Qi at Pinglu.

sentences.zh: 齊侯太公和立二年，和卒，子桓公午立。

translations.literal: In the second year after Marquis Tai Gong He of Qi had taken the throne, He died and his son Duke Huan Wu succeeded him.
translations.idiomatic: After Marquis Tai Gong He of Qi had reigned for two years, he died. His son Duke Huan, named Wu, succeeded him.

sentences.zh: 桓公午五年，秦、魏攻韓，韓求救於齊。

translations.literal: In the fifth year of Duke Huan Wu, Qin and Wei attacked Han. Han sought aid from Qi.
translations.idiomatic: In the fifth year of Duke Huan's reign, Qin and Wei attacked Han. Han turned to Qi for help.

sentences.zh: 齊桓公召大臣而謀曰：「蚤救之孰與晚救之？」

translations.literal: Duke Huan of Qi summoned his ministers to deliberate, saying: 'Is it better to rescue them early or late?'
translations.idiomatic: Duke Huan of Qi summoned his ministers to deliberate: "Is it better to come to their aid sooner or later?"

sentences.zh: 騶忌曰：「不若勿救。」

translations.literal: Zou Ji said: 'It would be better not to rescue them at all.'
translations.idiomatic: Zou Ji said: "It would be better not to rescue them at all."

sentences.zh: 段干朋曰：「不救，則韓且折而入於魏，不若救之。」

translations.literal: Duan Gan Peng said: 'If we do not rescue them, Han will be broken and absorbed by Wei. Better to rescue them.'
translations.idiomatic: Duan Gan Peng countered: "If we do not rescue them, Han will be broken and absorbed into Wei. Better to come to their aid."

sentences.zh: 田臣思曰：「過矣君之謀也！

translations.literal: Tian Chen Si said: 'My lord's plans miss the mark!'
translations.idiomatic: Tian Chensi spoke up: "My lord, your deliberations have missed the true opportunity!

sentences.zh: 秦、魏攻韓、楚，趙必救之，是天以燕予齊也。」

translations.literal: When Qin and Wei attack Han and Chu, Zhao will surely rescue them. This is Heaven giving Yan to Qi.
translations.idiomatic: When Qin and Wei are attacking Han and Chu, Zhao will surely rush to their rescue — this is Heaven handing Yan to Qi on a platter."

sentences.zh: 桓公曰：「善」。

translations.literal: Duke Huan said: 'Excellent.'
translations.idiomatic: Duke Huan said: "Excellent."

sentences.zh: 乃陰告韓使者而遣之。

translations.literal: He then secretly informed the Han envoy and sent him away.
translations.idiomatic: He then secretly assured the envoy from Han and sent him on his way.

sentences.zh: 韓自以為得齊之救，因與秦、魏戰。

translations.literal: Han believed it had secured Qi's rescue, and therefore went to war with Qin and Wei.
translations.idiomatic: Han, believing that Qi would come to its rescue, committed itself to battle against Qin and Wei.

sentences.zh: 楚、趙聞之，果起兵而救之。

translations.literal: Chu and Zhao, hearing this, indeed raised armies to rescue them.
translations.idiomatic: When Chu and Zhao learned of the fighting, they indeed raised their armies and went to the rescue.

sentences.zh: 齊因起兵襲燕國，取桑丘。

translations.literal: Qi then raised its army to raid the state of Yan and took Sangqiu.
translations.idiomatic: Qi seized the opportunity to launch a surprise attack on Yan and captured Sangqiu.

sentences.zh: 六年，救衛。

translations.literal: In the sixth year, they rescued Wei.
translations.idiomatic: In the sixth year, Qi went to the rescue of Wei.

sentences.zh: 桓公卒，子威王因齊立。

translations.literal: Duke Huan died; his son King Wei of Qi succeeded him.
translations.idiomatic: Duke Huan died and was succeeded by his son, who became King Wei of Qi.

sentences.zh: 是歲，故齊康公卒，絕無後，奉邑皆入田氏。

translations.literal: That year, the former Duke Kang of Qi died without heirs, and his sustenance fief all reverted to the Tian clan.
translations.idiomatic: That same year, the former Duke Kang of the old Qi ruling house died without an heir, and his sustenance fief reverted entirely to the Tian clan.

sentences.zh: 騶忌子以鼓琴見威王，威王說而捨之右室。

translations.literal: Zou Ji gained an audience with King Wei through playing the zither. King Wei was pleased and lodged him in the right chamber.
translations.idiomatic: Zou Ji gained an audience with King Wei by performing on the zither. The king was delighted and lodged him in the right chamber of the palace.

sentences.zh: 須臾，王鼓琴，騶忌子推戶入曰：「善哉鼓琴！」

translations.literal: Before long, the king played the zither. Zou Ji pushed open the door, entered, and said: 'How excellent, this zither playing!'
translations.idiomatic: Before long, the king began to play the zither. Zou Ji pushed open the door, strode in, and exclaimed: "What magnificent playing!"

sentences.zh: 王勃然不說，去琴按劍曰：「夫子見容未察，何以知其善也？」

translations.literal: The king suddenly grew displeased, set aside the zither, placed his hand on his sword, and said: 'Sir, you have scarcely looked and have not observed closely. How can you know it is good?'
translations.idiomatic: The king bristled with displeasure. He set aside the zither, placed his hand upon his sword, and demanded: "Sir, you have barely entered and hardly observed — how can you possibly know that it was good?"

sentences.zh: 騶忌子曰：「夫大弦濁以春溫者，君也；

translations.literal: Zou Ji said: 'The thick strings, deep and warm as spring, represent the sovereign.'
translations.idiomatic: Zou Ji replied: "The deep tones of the thick strings, warm as spring — those represent the sovereign."

sentences.zh: 小弦廉折以清者，相也；

translations.literal: The thin strings, sharp and clear, represent the chief minister.
translations.idiomatic: "The thin strings, crisp and clear in their turning — those represent the chief minister."

sentences.zh: 攫之深，醳之愉者，政令也；

translations.literal: Plucking deeply and releasing with ease represents the administration of government.
translations.idiomatic: "The deep pluck and the easy release — those represent the edicts of governance."

sentences.zh: 鈞諧以鳴，大小相益，回邪而不相害者，四時也：吾是以知其善也。」

translations.literal: The sounds being balanced and harmonious, large and small complementing each other, curving without harming one another — this represents the four seasons. This is how I know it is good.
translations.idiomatic: "When the tones are balanced and harmonious, the deep and the high complementing each other, the irregular notes flowing without disruption — that represents the four seasons. This is how I knew your playing was excellent."

sentences.zh: 王曰：「善語音。」

translations.literal: The king said: 'You speak well of music.'
translations.idiomatic: The king said: "You have a fine way of speaking about music."

sentences.zh: 騶忌子曰：「何獨語音，夫治國家而弭人民皆在其中。」

translations.literal: Zou Ji said: 'Why speak only of music? Governing the state and bringing peace to the people — all is contained within it.'
translations.idiomatic: Zou Ji responded: "Why limit this to music alone? The art of governing the state and bringing peace to the people is contained entirely within these same principles."

sentences.zh: 王又勃然不說曰：「若夫語五音之紀，信未有如夫子者也。

translations.literal: The king again grew displeased and said: 'When it comes to speaking of the principles of the five tones, truly none can match you, sir.'
translations.idiomatic: The king again grew irritated and said: "When it comes to discoursing on the principles of the five tones, I grant there is none to match you."

sentences.zh: 若夫治國家而弭人民，又何為乎絲桐之閒？」

translations.literal: But as for governing the state and bringing peace to the people, what has that to do with strings and wood?
translations.idiomatic: "But as for governing the state and pacifying the people — what has that to do with silk strings and paulownia wood?"

sentences.zh: 騶忌子曰：「夫大弦濁以春溫者，君也；

translations.literal: Zou Ji said: 'The thick strings, deep and warm as spring, represent the sovereign.'
translations.idiomatic: Zou Ji answered: "The thick strings, deep and warm as spring, represent the sovereign."

sentences.zh: 小弦廉折以清者，相也；

translations.literal: The thin strings, sharp and clear, represent the chief minister.
translations.idiomatic: "The thin strings, sharp and clear, represent the chief minister."

sentences.zh: 攫之深而捨之愉者，政令也；

translations.literal: Plucking deeply and releasing with ease represents the administration of government.
translations.idiomatic: "Plucking deeply and releasing with easy grace — that represents governance."

sentences.zh: 鈞諧以鳴，大小相益，回邪而不相害者，四時也。

translations.literal: The sounds being balanced and harmonious, large and small complementing each other, curving without harming one another — this represents the four seasons.
translations.idiomatic: "When the tones are balanced and harmonious, the deep and the high complementing each other, the irregular notes flowing without conflict — that represents the four seasons."

sentences.zh: 夫復而不亂者，所以治昌也；

translations.literal: Repeating without disorder is what brings good governance and prosperity.
translations.idiomatic: "When a melody repeats without falling into disorder, that is what brings about good governance and prosperity."

sentences.zh: 連而徑者，所以存亡也：故曰琴音調而天下治。

translations.literal: Connecting and going straight is what preserves what is perishing. Therefore it is said: when the zither is in tune, the realm is well governed.
translations.idiomatic: "When phrases flow seamlessly and reach their resolution, that is what preserves the state from ruin. Hence the saying: when the zither is in tune, all under Heaven is well governed."

sentences.zh: 夫治國家而弭人民者，無若乎五音者。」

translations.literal: For governing the state and bringing peace to the people, nothing compares to the five tones.
translations.idiomatic: "For the art of governing the state and bringing peace to the people, there is no better model than the five tones."

sentences.zh: 王曰：「善。」

translations.literal: The king said: 'Excellent.'
translations.idiomatic: The king said: "Excellent."

sentences.zh: 騶忌子見三月而受相印。

translations.literal: Within three months of his audience, Zou Ji received the seal of chief minister.
translations.idiomatic: Within three months of his first audience, Zou Ji received the seal of chief minister.

sentences.zh: 淳于髡見之曰：「善說哉！

translations.literal: Chunyu Kun met him and said: 'What a fine persuader you are!'
translations.idiomatic: Chunyu Kun called upon him and said: "What a masterful persuader you are!

sentences.zh: 髡有愚志，願陳諸前。」

translations.literal: Kun has some humble thoughts and wishes to lay them before you.
translations.idiomatic: I have a few humble observations that I would like to set before you."

sentences.zh: 騶忌子曰：「謹受教。」

translations.literal: Zou Ji said: 'I respectfully accept your instruction.'
translations.idiomatic: Zou Ji replied: "I respectfully await your instruction."

sentences.zh: 淳于髡曰：「得全全昌，失全全亡。」

translations.literal: Chunyu Kun said: 'Obtain completeness and all will flourish; lose completeness and all will perish.'
translations.idiomatic: Chunyu Kun said: "Preserve what is whole, and all shall prosper; lose what is whole, and all shall perish."

sentences.zh: 騶忌子曰：「謹受令，請謹毋離前。」

translations.literal: Zou Ji said: 'I respectfully accept your instruction. I shall take care never to stray from the sovereign's presence.'
translations.idiomatic: Zou Ji replied: "I accept your counsel. I shall take care never to stray from the sovereign's side."

sentences.zh: 淳于髡曰：「狶膏棘軸，所以為滑也，然而不能運方穿。」

translations.literal: Chunyu Kun said: 'Pig grease on a jujube axle makes it smooth, yet it cannot turn a square peg through a round hole.'
translations.idiomatic: Chunyu Kun continued: "Pig grease on a jujube axle makes it smooth, yet even so it cannot force a square peg through a round hole."

sentences.zh: 騶忌子曰：「謹受令，請謹事左右。」

translations.literal: Zou Ji said: 'I respectfully accept your instruction. I shall take care to serve those around the throne.'
translations.idiomatic: Zou Ji replied: "I accept your counsel. I shall take care to cultivate good relations with those close to the throne."

sentences.zh: 淳于髡曰：「弓膠昔干，所以為合也，然而不能傅合疏罅。」

translations.literal: Chunyu Kun said: 'Bow glue binds wood together, yet it cannot seal wide cracks.'
translations.idiomatic: Chunyu Kun said: "Bow glue bonds wood together, yet even it cannot seal wide gaps."

sentences.zh: 騶忌子曰：「謹受令，請謹自附於萬民。」

translations.literal: Zou Ji said: 'I respectfully accept your instruction. I shall take care to attach myself to the common people.'
translations.idiomatic: Zou Ji replied: "I accept your counsel. I shall take care to stay close to the common people."

sentences.zh: 淳于髡曰：「狐裘雖敝，不可補以黃狗之皮。」

translations.literal: Chunyu Kun said: 'Even when a fox fur coat is worn, it cannot be patched with the skin of a yellow dog.'
translations.idiomatic: Chunyu Kun said: "Even when a fox-fur robe is worn through, one must not patch it with the hide of a yellow dog."

sentences.zh: 騶忌子曰：「謹受令，請謹擇君子，毋雜小人其閒。」

translations.literal: Zou Ji said: 'I respectfully accept your instruction. I shall take care to select gentlemen and not mix petty men among them.'
translations.idiomatic: Zou Ji replied: "I accept your counsel. I shall take care to select men of virtue and never allow petty men to be mixed among them."

sentences.zh: 淳于髡曰：「大車不較，不能載其常任；

translations.literal: Chunyu Kun said: 'If a great cart is not calibrated, it cannot bear its usual load.'
translations.idiomatic: Chunyu Kun said: "If a great cart is not properly adjusted, it cannot bear its usual load."

sentences.zh: 琴瑟不較，不能成其五音。」

translations.literal: If a zither and lute are not calibrated, they cannot produce the five tones.
translations.idiomatic: "If a zither and a lute are not properly tuned, they cannot produce the five tones."

sentences.zh: 騶忌子曰：「謹受令，請謹修法律而督姦吏。」

translations.literal: Zou Ji said: 'I respectfully accept your instruction. I shall take care to refine the laws and supervise corrupt officials.'
translations.idiomatic: Zou Ji replied: "I accept your counsel. I shall take care to uphold the laws and root out corrupt officials."

sentences.zh: 淳于髡說畢，趨出，至門，而面其仆曰：「是人者，吾語之微言五，其應我若響之應聲，是人必封不久矣。」

translations.literal: When Chunyu Kun finished speaking, he hurried out. Reaching the gate, he turned to his servant and said: 'This man — I spoke to him in five veiled sayings, and his responses matched mine as echoes match sounds. This man will surely be enfeoffed before long.'
translations.idiomatic: When Chunyu Kun had finished, he strode briskly out. At the gate he turned to his attendant and said: "I tested that man with five veiled sayings, and his answers matched mine as perfectly as echoes follow sound. Mark my words — he will be enfeoffed before long."

sentences.zh: 居朞，封以下邳，號曰成侯。

translations.literal: After a year, he was enfeoffed at Xiapi and given the title Marquis Cheng.
translations.idiomatic: Within a year, Zou Ji was enfeoffed at Xiapi and granted the title Marquis Cheng.

sentences.zh: 威王二十三年，與趙王會平陸。

translations.literal: In the twenty-third year of King Wei, he met with the King of Zhao at Pinglu.
translations.idiomatic: In the twenty-third year of King Wei's reign, he met with the King of Zhao at Pinglu.

sentences.zh: 二十四年，與魏王會田於郊。

translations.literal: In the twenty-fourth year, he met with the King of Wei to hunt in the outskirts.
translations.idiomatic: In the twenty-fourth year, he met with the King of Wei for a hunt in the countryside.

sentences.zh: 魏王問曰：「王亦有寶乎？」

translations.literal: The King of Wei asked: 'Does Your Majesty also possess treasures?'
translations.idiomatic: The King of Wei asked: "Does Your Majesty also possess treasures?"

sentences.zh: 威王曰：「無有。」

translations.literal: King Wei said: 'I have none.'
translations.idiomatic: King Wei replied: "I have none."

sentences.zh: 梁王曰：「若寡人國小也，尚有徑寸之珠照車前後各十二乘者十枚，奈何以萬乘之國而無寶乎？」

translations.literal: The King of Liang said: 'Even my small state possesses ten pearls an inch in diameter that illuminate twelve chariots before and behind. How can a state of ten thousand chariots have no treasures?'
translations.idiomatic: The King of Liang said: "Even a small state such as mine possesses ten pearls, each an inch across, whose radiance lights up twelve chariots fore and aft. How is it possible that a state of ten thousand chariots has no treasures?"

sentences.zh: 威王曰：「寡人之所以為寶與王異。

translations.literal: King Wei said: 'What I consider treasures differs from what Your Majesty regards as such.'
translations.idiomatic: King Wei replied: "What I regard as treasures is quite different from what Your Majesty has in mind."

sentences.zh: 吾臣有檀子者，使守南城，則楚人不敢為寇東取，泗上十二諸侯皆來朝。

translations.literal: Among my ministers is Tanzi. I stationed him to guard Nancheng, and the men of Chu dare not raid our eastern lands. The twelve lords along the Si River all come to pay court.
translations.idiomatic: "Among my ministers there is one called Tanzi. I stationed him to guard Nancheng, and since then the men of Chu have not dared to raid our eastern border. The twelve lords along the Si River all come to pay us court."

sentences.zh: 吾臣有子者，使守高唐，則趙人不敢東漁於河。

translations.literal: Among my ministers is Panzi. I stationed him to guard Gaotang, and the men of Zhao dare not fish eastward in the Yellow River.
translations.idiomatic: "Another of my ministers is Panzi. I stationed him to guard Gaotang, and since then the men of Zhao have not dared to fish the eastern stretches of the Yellow River."

sentences.zh: 吾吏有黔夫者，使守徐州，則燕人祭北門，趙人祭西門，徙而從者七千餘家。

translations.literal: Among my officials is Qian Fu. I stationed him to guard Xuzhou, and the men of Yan pray at the north gate while the men of Zhao pray at the west gate; over seven thousand households have migrated to follow him.
translations.idiomatic: "I have an official named Qian Fu who guards Xuzhou. Under his watch, the people of Yan pray for protection at the north gate and the people of Zhao at the west gate. More than seven thousand households have relocated to live under his administration."

sentences.zh: 吾臣有種首者，使備盜賊，則道不拾遺。

translations.literal: Among my ministers is Zhong Shou. I ordered him to guard against thieves, and as a result no one on the roads picks up lost items.
translations.idiomatic: "And I have a minister named Zhong Shou, whom I charged with preventing banditry. Under his watch, no one on the roads dares pick up what is left behind."

sentences.zh: 將以照千里，豈特十二乘哉！」

translations.literal: These shall illuminate a thousand li — how can they merely light twelve chariots!
translations.idiomatic: "These treasures of mine illuminate a thousand li. How can they be compared to merely lighting twelve chariots?"

sentences.zh: 梁惠王慚，不懌而去。

translations.literal: King Hui of Liang was ashamed, departed unhappily.
translations.idiomatic: King Hui of Liang was left speechless with shame. He departed in ill humor.

sentences.zh: 二十六年，魏惠王圍邯鄲，趙求救於齊。

translations.literal: In the twenty-sixth year, King Hui of Wei besieged Handan. Zhao sought aid from Qi.
translations.idiomatic: In the twenty-sixth year, King Hui of Wei laid siege to Handan, and Zhao turned to Qi for help.

sentences.zh: 齊威王召大臣而謀曰：「救趙孰與勿救？」

translations.literal: King Wei of Qi summoned his ministers to deliberate: 'Is it better to rescue Zhao or not?'
translations.idiomatic: King Wei of Qi summoned his ministers to deliberate: "Shall we rescue Zhao, or not?"

sentences.zh: 騶忌子曰：「不如勿救。」

translations.literal: Zou Ji said: 'It would be better not to rescue them.'
translations.idiomatic: Zou Ji said: "It would be better not to rescue them."

sentences.zh: 段干朋曰：「不救則不義，且不利。」

translations.literal: Duan Gan Peng said: 'Not to rescue them would be both unrighteous and disadvantageous.'
translations.idiomatic: Duan Gan Peng countered: "Not to rescue them would be both unjust and contrary to our interests."

sentences.zh: 威王曰：「何也？」

translations.literal: King Wei asked: 'Why?'
translations.idiomatic: King Wei asked: "How so?"

sentences.zh: 對曰：「夫魏氏并邯鄲，其於齊何利哉？

translations.literal: He replied: 'If Wei annexes Handan, how does that benefit Qi?'
translations.idiomatic: Duan Gan Peng replied: "If Wei annexes Handan, what benefit would that bring to Qi?

sentences.zh: 且夫救趙而軍其郊，是趙不伐而魏全也。

translations.literal: Moreover, if we rescue Zhao but merely station our army at its borders, Zhao is not attacked but Wei remains whole.
translations.idiomatic: Furthermore, if we rescue Zhao but merely station our army at its borders, then Zhao is spared the attack but Wei emerges unscathed.

sentences.zh: 故不如南攻襄陵以獘魏，邯鄲拔而乘魏之獘。」

translations.literal: Therefore it would be better to attack Xiangling to the south to exhaust Wei, and then after Handan falls, exploit Wei's exhaustion.
translations.idiomatic: Better to attack Xiangling in the south to weaken Wei, and then, once Handan has fallen, exploit Wei's exhaustion."

sentences.zh: 威王從其計。

translations.literal: King Wei followed his plan.
translations.idiomatic: King Wei adopted his strategy.

sentences.zh: 其後成侯騶忌與田忌不善，公孫閱謂成侯忌曰：「公何不謀伐魏，田忌必將。

translations.literal: Later, Marquis Cheng Zou Ji and Tian Ji were on bad terms. Gongsun Yue said to Marquis Cheng: 'Why not propose an attack on Wei? Tian Ji will certainly be made the general.'
translations.idiomatic: Later, Marquis Cheng Zou Ji fell into rivalry with Tian Ji. Gongsun Yue counseled Marquis Cheng: "Why not propose an attack on Wei? Tian Ji will certainly be appointed to lead the campaign."

sentences.zh: 戰勝有功，則公之謀中也；

translations.literal: If the battle is won and merit is gained, then your plan will be proved right.
translations.idiomatic: "If he wins the battle and earns merit, your plan will be credited for the success."

sentences.zh: 戰不勝，非前死則後北，而命在公矣。」

translations.literal: If the battle is lost, he will either die in the front or flee in the rear, and his fate will be in your hands.
translations.idiomatic: "If he loses, he will either die on the front line or be disgraced by retreat, and his fate will be in your hands."

sentences.zh: 於是成侯言威王，使田忌南攻襄陵。

translations.literal: Thereupon Marquis Cheng spoke to King Wei, who ordered Tian Ji to attack Xiangling to the south.
translations.idiomatic: Marquis Cheng accordingly spoke to King Wei, who then dispatched Tian Ji to attack Xiangling in the south.

sentences.zh: 十月，邯鄲拔，齊因起兵擊魏，大敗之桂陵。

translations.literal: In the tenth month, Handan fell. Qi then raised its army to attack Wei and inflicted a great defeat at Guiling.
translations.idiomatic: In the tenth month, Handan fell. Qi then launched its forces against Wei and dealt them a crushing defeat at Guiling.

sentences.zh: 于是齊最彊于諸侯，自稱為王，以令天下。

translations.literal: At this point Qi was the strongest among the feudal lords, proclaimed itself a kingdom, and issued commands to the realm.
translations.idiomatic: At this point Qi was the most powerful of all the feudal states. It proclaimed itself a kingdom and issued commands to all under Heaven.

sentences.zh: 三十三年，殺其大夫牟辛。

translations.literal: In the thirty-third year, they killed their grandee Mou Xin.
translations.idiomatic: In the thirty-third year, the grandee Mou Xin was put to death.

sentences.zh: 三十五年，公孫閱又謂成侯忌曰：「公何不令人操十金卜於市，曰『我田忌之人也。

translations.literal: In the thirty-fifth year, Gongsun Yue again spoke to Marquis Cheng Zou Ji: 'Why not have someone take ten pieces of gold and consult a diviner in the marketplace, saying: "I am a man of Tian Ji's."'
translations.idiomatic: In the thirty-fifth year, Gongsun Yue again counseled Marquis Cheng: "Why not have someone take ten pieces of gold to a diviner in the marketplace and say: 'I serve Tian Ji.'

sentences.zh: 吾三戰而三勝，聲威天下。

translations.literal: I have fought three battles and won three victories, my fame resounding throughout the realm.
translations.idiomatic: "'I have fought three battles and won three victories. My fame resounds throughout the realm.'

sentences.zh: 欲為大事，亦吉乎不吉乎』？」

translations.literal: I wish to undertake a great enterprise — is it auspicious or not?
translations.idiomatic: "'I wish to undertake a great enterprise — will the omens be auspicious or not?'"

sentences.zh: 卜者出，因令人捕為之卜者，驗其辭於王之所。

translations.literal: When the diviner emerged, he then had someone arrest the diviner and bring his words before the king as evidence.
translations.idiomatic: When the diviner emerged, Gongsun Yue had him arrested and brought the diviner's words before the king as evidence of Tian Ji's treasonous ambitions.

sentences.zh: 田忌聞之，因率其徒襲攻臨淄，求成侯，不勝而奔。

translations.literal: When Tian Ji heard of this, he led his followers in a surprise attack on Linzi, seeking Marquis Cheng. He did not succeed and fled.
translations.idiomatic: When Tian Ji learned of the plot, he led his followers in a surprise attack on the capital Linzi, seeking to seize Marquis Cheng. The attack failed, and Tian Ji was forced to flee.

sentences.zh: 三十六年，威王卒，子宣王辟彊立。

translations.literal: In the thirty-sixth year, King Wei died. His son King Xuan Bi Jiang succeeded him.
translations.idiomatic: In the thirty-sixth year, King Wei died and was succeeded by his son, King Xuan, whose personal name was Bi Jiang.

sentences.zh: 二年，魏伐趙。

translations.literal: In the second year, Wei attacked Zhao.
translations.idiomatic: In the second year of King Xuan's reign, Wei attacked Zhao.

sentences.zh: 趙與韓親，共擊魏。

translations.literal: Zhao and Han were allies and jointly attacked Wei.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao and Han, being allies, jointly attacked Wei.

sentences.zh: 趙不利，戰於南梁。

translations.literal: Zhao fared poorly; they fought at Nanliang.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao met with setbacks and was engaged at Nanliang.

sentences.zh: 宣王召田忌復故位。

translations.literal: King Xuan summoned Tian Ji and restored him to his former position.
translations.idiomatic: King Xuan recalled Tian Ji and restored him to his former position.

sentences.zh: 韓氏請救於齊。

translations.literal: Han sought aid from Qi.
translations.idiomatic: Han appealed to Qi for assistance.

sentences.zh: 宣王召大臣而謀曰：「蚤救孰與晚救？」

translations.literal: King Xuan summoned his ministers to deliberate: 'Is it better to rescue them early or late?'
translations.idiomatic: King Xuan summoned his ministers and asked: "Is it better to come to their aid sooner or later?"

sentences.zh: 騶忌子曰：「不如勿救。」

translations.literal: Zou Ji said: 'It would be better not to rescue them.'
translations.idiomatic: Zou Ji advised: "It would be better not to rescue them."

sentences.zh: 田忌曰：「弗救，則韓且折而入於魏，不如蚤救之。」

translations.literal: Tian Ji said: 'If we do not rescue them, Han will be broken and absorbed by Wei. Better to rescue them early.'
translations.idiomatic: Tian Ji argued: "If we do not rescue them, Han will be broken and absorbed by Wei. Better to act sooner."

sentences.zh: 孫子曰：「夫韓、魏之兵未獘而救之，是吾代韓受魏之兵，顧反聽命於韓也。

translations.literal: Sunzi said: 'If we rescue Han before the troops of Han and Wei are exhausted, we would be substituting ourselves for Han to receive Wei's attack, and in the end we would be taking orders from Han.'
translations.idiomatic: Sun Bin said: "If we rescue Han before the armies of Han and Wei are exhausted, we would simply be taking Han's place in bearing Wei's assault, and in the end we would find ourselves at Han's beck and call.

sentences.zh: 且魏有破國之志，韓見亡，必東面而愬於齊矣。

translations.literal: Moreover, Wei has the ambition to destroy a state. When Han sees itself on the verge of ruin, it will surely face east and plead with Qi.
translations.idiomatic: Moreover, Wei intends to destroy Han entirely. When Han sees ruin staring it in the face, it will inevitably turn eastward and throw itself upon Qi's mercy.

sentences.zh: 吾因深結韓之親而晚承魏之獘，則可重利而得尊名也。」

translations.literal: If we deeply secure Han's allegiance and wait to exploit Wei's exhaustion, then we can gain double the advantage and win an honored name.
translations.idiomatic: If we bind Han firmly to our cause and wait to strike when Wei is exhausted, we can reap a double advantage and earn an honored name."

sentences.zh: 宣王曰：「善。」

translations.literal: King Xuan said: 'Excellent.'
translations.idiomatic: King Xuan said: "Excellent."

sentences.zh: 乃陰告韓之使者而遣之。

translations.literal: He then secretly informed the envoy from Han and sent him away.
translations.idiomatic: He then secretly assured the envoy from Han and sent him on his way.

sentences.zh: 韓因恃齊，五戰不勝，而東委國於齊。

translations.literal: Han relied on Qi but lost five battles, and then turned east to entrust its state to Qi.
translations.idiomatic: Relying on Qi's promise, Han fought five battles and lost every one. It then turned eastward and placed its fate in Qi's hands.

sentences.zh: 齊因起兵，使田忌、田嬰將，孫子為（帥）［師］，救韓、趙以擊魏，大敗之馬陵，殺其將龐涓，虜魏太子申。

translations.literal: Qi then raised its army, with Tian Ji and Tian Ying as generals and Sunzi as military adviser, to rescue Han and Zhao and attack Wei. They dealt Wei a crushing defeat at Maling, killed their general Pang Juan, and captured Crown Prince Shen of Wei.
translations.idiomatic: Qi then raised its army, appointing Tian Ji and Tian Ying as generals with Sun Bin as military adviser, to rescue Han and Zhao and strike against Wei. They inflicted a devastating defeat on Wei at Maling, slew the Wei general Pang Juan, and captured Crown Prince Shen of Wei.

sentences.zh: 其後三晉之王皆因田嬰朝齊王於博望，盟而去。

translations.literal: Afterward, the kings of the Three Jin all came through Tian Ying to pay court to the King of Qi at Bowang, swore oaths, and departed.
translations.idiomatic: Afterward, the kings of the Three Jin states all came through Tian Ying to pay court to the King of Qi at Bowang, swore oaths of alliance, and departed.

sentences.zh: 七年，與魏王會平阿南。

translations.literal: In the seventh year, he met with the King of Wei at Ping'a South.
translations.idiomatic: In the seventh year, King Xuan met with the King of Wei south of Ping'a.

sentences.zh: 明年，復會甄。

translations.literal: The following year, they met again at Zhen.
translations.idiomatic: The following year, they met again at Zhen.

sentences.zh: 魏惠王卒。

translations.literal: King Hui of Wei died.
translations.idiomatic: King Hui of Wei died.

sentences.zh: 明年，與魏襄王會徐州，諸侯相王也。

translations.literal: The following year, he met with King Xiang of Wei at Xuzhou; the feudal lords mutually acknowledged each other as kings.
translations.idiomatic: The following year, King Xuan met with King Xiang of Wei at Xuzhou, where the feudal lords formally acknowledged one another as kings.

sentences.zh: 十年，楚圍我徐州。

translations.literal: In the tenth year, Chu besieged our Xuzhou.
translations.idiomatic: In the tenth year, Chu laid siege to Xuzhou.

sentences.zh: 十一年，與魏伐趙，趙決河水灌齊、魏，兵罷。

translations.literal: In the eleventh year, they attacked Zhao together with Wei. Zhao breached the Yellow River to flood the armies of Qi and Wei, and the campaign was abandoned.
translations.idiomatic: In the eleventh year, Qi joined with Wei in attacking Zhao. Zhao broke the dikes of the Yellow River to flood the armies of Qi and Wei, and the campaign was called off.

sentences.zh: 十八年，宣王喜文學游說之士，自如騶衍、淳于髡、田駢、接子、慎到、環淵之徒七十六人，皆賜列第，為上大夫，不治而議論。

translations.literal: In the eighteenth year, King Xuan delighted in men of learning and persuasion. Scholars such as Zou Yan, Chunyu Kun, Tian Pian, Jiezi, Shen Dao, and Huan Yuan — seventy-six in all — were all granted residences and appointed as senior grandees, deliberating on affairs without holding administrative posts.
translations.idiomatic: In the eighteenth year, King Xuan, who delighted in scholars and rhetoricians, gathered men such as Zou Yan, Chunyu Kun, Tian Pian, Jiezi, Shen Dao, and Huan Yuan — seventy-six in all. He granted each of them a residence, appointed them as senior grandees, and charged them with deliberating on matters of state without holding administrative office.

sentences.zh: 是以齊稷下學士復盛，且數百千人。

translations.literal: As a result, the scholars at the Jixia Academy of Qi flourished once again, numbering several hundred to over a thousand.
translations.idiomatic: As a result, the scholars at the Jixia Academy flourished once again, their number swelling to several hundred and even thousands.

sentences.zh: 十九年，宣王卒，子湣王地立。

translations.literal: In the nineteenth year, King Xuan died. His son King Min Di succeeded him.
translations.idiomatic: In the nineteenth year, King Xuan died. His son, King Min, whose personal name was Di, succeeded him.

sentences.zh: 十二年，攻魏。

translations.literal: In the twelfth year, they attacked Wei.
translations.idiomatic: In the twelfth year of King Min's reign, Qi attacked Wei.

sentences.zh: 楚圍雍氏，秦敗屈丐。

translations.literal: Chu besieged Yongshi. Qin defeated Qu Gai.
translations.idiomatic: At the same time, Chu besieged the city of Yongshi, and Qin defeated the Chu general Qu Gai.

sentences.zh: 蘇代謂田軫曰：「臣願有謁於公，其為事甚完，使楚利公，成為福，不成亦為福。

translations.literal: Su Dai said to Tian Zhen: 'Your servant wishes to present something to you. The plan is very thorough: if Chu benefits you, success is fortunate, and failure is also fortunate.'
translations.idiomatic: Su Dai addressed Tian Zhen: "I have a proposal for you, my lord. The plan is airtight: whether it succeeds or fails, it will work to your advantage in relation to Chu."

sentences.zh: 今者臣立於門，客有言曰魏王謂韓馮、張儀曰：『煮棗將拔，齊兵又進，子來救寡人則可矣；

translations.literal: Today as I stood at the gate, a guest said that the King of Wei told Han Feng and Zhang Yi: 'Zaozao is about to fall, and Qi's army advances further. If you come to rescue me, it will be sufficient.'
translations.idiomatic: "Today, as I stood at the gate, a visitor reported that the King of Wei told Han Feng and Zhang Yi: 'Zaozao is about to fall, and the army of Qi presses forward. Come to my rescue, and all will be well.'"

sentences.zh: 不救寡人，寡人弗能拔。』

translations.literal: 'If you do not rescue me, I will not be able to hold out.'
translations.idiomatic: "'If you do not rescue me, I shall not be able to hold out.'"

sentences.zh: 此特轉辭也。

translations.literal: This is merely a diversionary statement.
translations.idiomatic: "But this is merely a diplomatic ploy."

sentences.zh: 秦、韓之兵毋東，旬餘，則魏氏轉韓從秦，秦逐張儀，交臂而事齊楚，此公之事成也。」

translations.literal: If the troops of Qin and Han do not move east, then within ten days Wei will shift Han to follow Qin, Qin will send Zhang Yi away, and they will join together to serve Qi and Chu — then your affairs will be accomplished.
translations.idiomatic: "If the troops of Qin and Han do not march east, then within ten days Wei will detach Han from Qin's cause, Qin will dismiss Zhang Yi, and they will all clasp hands to serve Qi and Chu. Then your designs will be realized."

sentences.zh: 田軫曰：「柰何使無東？」

translations.literal: Tian Zhen asked: 'How can we prevent them from marching east?'
translations.idiomatic: Tian Zhen asked: "But how can we prevent them from marching east?"

sentences.zh: 對曰：「韓馮之救魏之辭，必不謂韓王曰『馮以為魏』，必曰『馮將以秦韓之兵東卻齊宋，馮因摶三國之兵，乘屈丐之獘，南割於楚，故地必盡得之矣』。

translations.literal: He replied: 'When Han Feng argues for rescuing Wei, he will certainly not tell the King of Han "Feng does this for Wei's sake." He will surely say: "Feng will use the troops of Qin and Han to drive Qi and Song eastward, then unite the three states' armies, exploit Qu Gai's defeat, and carve territory from Chu to the south — all the former lands will be recovered."'
translations.idiomatic: Su Dai replied: "When Han Feng makes his case for rescuing Wei, he will never tell the King of Han, 'I am doing this for Wei.' Instead, he will say: 'I shall lead the combined forces of Qin and Han eastward to repel Qi and Song. Then I shall unite the armies of three states, exploit Qu Gai's defeat, and carve territory from Chu in the south. All the old lands shall surely be recovered.'"

sentences.zh: 張儀救魏之辭，必不謂秦王曰『儀以為魏』，必曰『儀且以秦韓之兵東距齊宋，儀將摶三國之兵，乘屈丐之獘，南割於楚，名存亡國，實伐三川而歸，此王業也』。

translations.literal: Zhang Yi's argument for rescuing Wei will certainly not tell the King of Qin 'Yi does this for Wei.' He will surely say: 'Yi will lead the troops of Qin and Han east to repel Qi and Song, then unite the three states' armies, exploit Qu Gai's defeat, carve territory from Chu in the south — in name saving a perishing state, in reality attacking Sanchuan and returning. This is a royal enterprise.'
translations.idiomatic: "Likewise, when Zhang Yi argues for rescuing Wei, he will never tell the King of Qin, 'I am doing this for Wei.' He will say: 'I shall lead the forces of Qin and Han eastward to block Qi and Song, then unite the three states' armies, exploit Qu Gai's defeat, and seize territory from Chu. In name, we save a perishing state; in reality, we conquer Sanchuan and return in triumph. This is the enterprise of a true king.'"

sentences.zh: 公令楚王與韓氏地，使秦制和，謂秦王曰『請與韓地，而王以施三川，韓氏之兵不用而得地於楚』。

translations.literal: If you have the King of Chu give land to Han and let Qin mediate the peace, saying to the King of Qin: 'Please give land to Han, and the King can bestow his favor upon Sanchuan. Han's troops need not be used and they will gain territory from Chu.'
translations.idiomatic: "If you persuade the King of Chu to cede land to Han and let Qin broker the peace, saying to the King of Qin: 'Grant land to Han, and Your Majesty may extend your influence over Sanchuan. Han's armies need not march, and yet they gain territory from Chu.'"

sentences.zh: 韓馮之東兵之辭且謂秦何？

translations.literal: What will Han Feng say to Qin in his argument for marching the troops east?
translations.idiomatic: "And what will Han Feng say to Qin when he argues for sending troops east?"

sentences.zh: 曰『秦兵不用而得三川，伐楚韓以窘魏，魏氏不敢東，是孤齊也』。

translations.literal: He will say: 'Qin gains Sanchuan without using its troops, attacks Chu and Han to corner Wei, Wei dares not move east — this isolates Qi.'
translations.idiomatic: "He will say: 'Qin gains Sanchuan without deploying a single soldier, while attacking Chu and Han corners Wei. Wei will not dare move east — and Qi will be isolated.'"

sentences.zh: 張儀之東兵之辭且謂何？

translations.literal: What will Zhang Yi say in his argument for marching troops east?
translations.idiomatic: "And what will Zhang Yi say in his argument for sending troops east?"

sentences.zh: 曰『秦韓欲地而兵有案，聲威發於魏，魏氏之欲不失齊楚者有資矣』。

translations.literal: He will say: 'Qin and Han desire territory while their troops stand ready. The prestige and power are directed at Wei; Wei's desire not to lose Qi and Chu will have resources to rely on.'
translations.idiomatic: "He will say: 'Qin and Han desire territory while keeping their armies in reserve. Their military prestige is directed at Wei, and Wei's desire to maintain ties with Qi and Chu will have the leverage it needs.'"

sentences.zh: 魏氏轉秦韓爭事齊楚，楚王欲而無與地，公令秦韓之兵不用而得地，有一大德也。

translations.literal: Wei will then shift Qin and Han into competing to serve Qi and Chu. The King of Chu wishes this but has given no territory. You enable Qin and Han to gain territory without using their troops — this is one great favor.
translations.idiomatic: "Wei will then redirect Qin and Han into competing to serve Qi and Chu. The King of Chu desires this outcome but has offered no territory. If you enable Qin and Han to acquire territory without deploying their armies, that will be an immense favor."

sentences.zh: 秦韓之王劫於韓馮、張儀而東兵以徇服魏，公常執左券以責於秦韓，此其善於公而惡張子多資矣。」

translations.literal: If the kings of Qin and Han are pressured by Han Feng and Zhang Yi to march east and coerce Wei into submission, you will always hold the left half of the tally to make demands upon Qin and Han. This benefits you greatly while working against Zhang Yi.
translations.idiomatic: "If the kings of Qin and Han are pressured by Han Feng and Zhang Yi to march east and coerce Wei, you will always hold the tally against Qin and Han. This arrangement greatly favors you while undermining Zhang Yi."

sentences.zh: 十三年，秦惠王卒。

translations.literal: In the thirteenth year, King Hui of Qin died.
translations.idiomatic: In the thirteenth year, King Hui of Qin died.

sentences.zh: 二十三年，與秦擊敗楚於重丘。

translations.literal: In the twenty-third year, they joined with Qin to defeat Chu at Chongqiu.
translations.idiomatic: In the twenty-third year, Qi joined Qin in defeating Chu at Chongqiu.

sentences.zh: 二十四年，秦使涇陽君質於齊。

translations.literal: In the twenty-fourth year, Qin sent Lord Jingyang as a hostage to Qi.
translations.idiomatic: In the twenty-fourth year, Qin sent Lord Jingyang to Qi as a hostage.

sentences.zh: 二十五年，歸涇陽君于秦。

translations.literal: In the twenty-fifth year, they returned Lord Jingyang to Qin.
translations.idiomatic: In the twenty-fifth year, Lord Jingyang was returned to Qin.

sentences.zh: 孟嘗君薛文入秦，即相秦。

translations.literal: Lord Mengchang, Xue Wen, entered Qin and immediately became chief minister of Qin.
translations.idiomatic: Lord Mengchang of Xue, named Wen, traveled to Qin and was appointed chief minister.

sentences.zh: 文亡去。

translations.literal: Wen fled and departed.
translations.idiomatic: Wen eventually fled and escaped.

sentences.zh: 二十六年，齊與韓魏共攻秦，至函谷軍焉。

translations.literal: In the twenty-sixth year, Qi joined with Han and Wei to attack Qin, reaching Hangu Pass with their forces.
translations.idiomatic: In the twenty-sixth year, Qi joined Han and Wei in an attack on Qin, advancing their armies as far as Hangu Pass.

sentences.zh: 二十八年，秦與韓河外以和，兵罷。

translations.literal: In the twenty-eighth year, Qin made peace with Han by ceding lands beyond the Yellow River, and the armies disbanded.
translations.idiomatic: In the twenty-eighth year, Qin made peace with Han by ceding territory beyond the Yellow River, and the armies were withdrawn.

sentences.zh: 二十九年，趙殺其主父。

translations.literal: In the twenty-ninth year, Zhao killed its Master Father.
translations.idiomatic: In the twenty-ninth year, Zhao killed its Master Father.

sentences.zh: 齊佐趙滅中山。

translations.literal: Qi assisted Zhao in destroying Zhongshan.
translations.idiomatic: Qi assisted Zhao in the destruction of Zhongshan.

sentences.zh: 三十六年，王為東帝，秦昭王為西帝。

translations.literal: In the thirty-sixth year, the King took the title Eastern Emperor, and King Zhao of Qin took the title Western Emperor.
translations.idiomatic: In the thirty-sixth year, the King of Qi assumed the title of Eastern Emperor, while King Zhao of Qin took the title of Western Emperor.

sentences.zh: 蘇代自燕來，入齊，見於章華東門。

translations.literal: Su Dai came from Yan, entered Qi, and was received at the Eastern Gate of Zhanghua.
translations.idiomatic: Su Dai arrived from Yan, entered Qi, and was received at the Eastern Gate of Zhanghua.

sentences.zh: 齊王曰：「嘻，善，子來！

translations.literal: The King of Qi said: 'Ah, good, you have come!'
translations.idiomatic: The King of Qi exclaimed: "Ah, how good that you have come!

sentences.zh: 秦使魏冉致帝，子以為何如？」

translations.literal: Qin has sent Wei Ran to confer the title of Emperor. What do you think?
translations.idiomatic: Qin has sent Wei Ran to confer the title of Emperor upon me. What do you think?"

sentences.zh: 對曰：「王之問臣也卒，而患之所從來微，願王受之而勿備稱也。

translations.literal: He replied: 'Your Majesty's question is sudden, and the source of the trouble is subtle. I would advise Your Majesty to accept it but refrain from using the title.'
translations.idiomatic: Su Dai replied: "Your Majesty's question comes suddenly, and the danger is more subtle than it appears. I would advise Your Majesty to accept the title but refrain from actually using it."

sentences.zh: 秦稱之，天下安之，王乃稱之，無後也。

translations.literal: If Qin uses the title and the world accepts it, then Your Majesty may use it too, without disadvantage.
translations.idiomatic: "If Qin uses the title and the world accepts it, then Your Majesty may safely adopt it as well, without consequence."

sentences.zh: 且讓爭帝名，無傷也。

translations.literal: Moreover, yielding the contention over the title of Emperor does no harm.
translations.idiomatic: "Moreover, yielding the contest over the imperial title does no harm."

sentences.zh: 秦稱之，天下惡之，王因勿稱，以收天下，此大資也。

translations.literal: If Qin uses the title and the world resents it, Your Majesty may refrain and thereby win the world — this is a great asset.
translations.idiomatic: "But if Qin uses the title and the world resents it, then Your Majesty may decline the title and win the hearts of all under Heaven. This is an enormous advantage."

sentences.zh: 且天下立兩帝，王以天下為尊齊乎？
